title: Ordering by Relevance vs Recency
concept: Deciding whether the most relevant or the most recent information should be placed closest to the query.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why do we remove near-duplicate passages from a context window?

options.text: Because duplicates waste tokens without adding new information
Because the model gets confused by any repeated word
Because duplicates make the text file too large to save

exercises.explain: Right! Deduplication frees up token space by cutting redundant passages, leaving more room for genuinely useful information — which sets up today's question: once you've trimmed the fat, how do you ORDER what's left?

cards.title: Newest Isn't Always Best
cards.body: Imagine asking a friend for advice, and they only remember the last thing you said — not the important thing you said five minutes ago. That's what happens when an LLM's context is ordered purely by time instead of by what actually matters.

cards.title: Two Ways to Order Context
cards.body: Recency ordering puts the newest information closest to the query, assuming 'latest = most important.' Relevance ordering puts the information most related to the actual question closest, no matter how old it is. Good context design usually needs to choose — or blend — both.

cards.title: Your Desk Before a Test
cards.body: When you cram, you put the notes you'll need FIRST right on top, closest to your hand — not necessarily the notes you wrote most recently. A model reading its context window works the same way: what's physically closest to the question gets the most attention.

cards.title: Medical Chatbot Example
cards.body: A patient asks 'what's my current dosage?' The chat history has 20 old messages plus one recent lab result. If you order by recency, the lab result naturally lands last (good!). But if the most relevant fact — the dosage change from 3 weeks ago — is buried under recent small talk, relevance ordering pulls it forward instead.

cards.title: Myth: Most Recent = Most Useful
cards.body: It's tempting to think the last message a user sent is always the most important context. But often an old document, a pinned instruction, or an early clarification is far more relevant than whatever was just said — recency and relevance are NOT the same thing.

cards.title: Relevance vs Recency, Side by Side
cards.body: Neither strategy is 'wrong' — they answer different questions. The trick is picking the one (or blend) that matches what your query actually needs.

left.text: Newest message closest to query. Great for live chats, breaking news, ongoing conversations.

right.text: Most on-topic passage closest to query. Great for search, Q&A over documents, fact lookups.

exercises.prompt: A news-summary bot needs to answer 'what happened in the game TODAY?' Which ordering strategy fits best here?

options.text: Recency ordering, since the newest game report is what's needed
Relevance ordering based on a game from 3 years ago
Random ordering to keep things fair

exercises.explain: When a query is inherently about 'what's newest,' recency ordering is actually the most relevant strategy — the newest info IS the most relevant info here.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Relevance ordering and recency ordering will always produce the exact same arrangement of context.
exercises.explain: False — they often disagree. An old document can be highly relevant while a brand-new message might be totally irrelevant. That's exactly why engineers must choose (or blend) a strategy on purpose.

exercises.sentence: Placing the most important passage closest to the query, regardless of when it was retrieved, is called ordering by relevance.
exercises.explain: Ordering by relevance means the score of how related a passage is to the query decides placement, not its timestamp.

exercises.prompt: Which of these context-ordering explanations sounds AI-generated (overly generic, buzzword-padded, lacking a concrete example)?

options.text: Leveraging a synergistic paradigm, context ordering optimizes information architecture to holistically enhance model comprehension outcomes.
We put the troubleshooting doc right before the question because it directly answers what the user asked, even though it's older than the chat log.
Recency ordering means newest messages go last, closest to the question, like putting the most recent sticky note on top of a pile.

exercises.explain: Option A is full of vague buzzwords ('synergistic paradigm', 'holistically enhance') with zero concrete example — a classic sign of AI-generated filler text, unlike the other two which give specific, grounded reasoning.

exercises.prompt: You're building a customer support bot. Context window has: (1) the customer's message from 2 seconds ago asking 'is it still broken?', (2) a highly relevant troubleshooting doc from last month, (3) a random old greeting message, (4) today's relevant bug report. How should you order the context, closest to the final query?

options.text: Strict chronological order, oldest first, newest last, no exceptions
Put the two most relevant items (troubleshooting doc, bug report) closest to the query, drop the irrelevant greeting
Put the random greeting closest since it's friendly and sets tone
Randomize the order so the model doesn't get biased

exercises.explain: The winning move blends both ideas: recency matters (today's bug report beats last month's doc if tied), but relevance decides who gets a seat at all — the irrelevant greeting gets cut, and truly useful info sits closest to the query where attention is strongest. This wraps up the unit: a well-structured context window isn't just deduplicated and trimmed, it's deliberately ordered so the most important information sits where the model is most likely to use it. That's the last core skill for structuring context windows — nice work leveling up your LLM application engineering toolkit!
